planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall (September–October) or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; fall planting in zone 9 allows establishment before the plant blooms and coincides with its peak growing period.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Setsugekka is widely regarded as one of the finest and most beloved sasanqua camellia cultivars, producing large (3–4-inch), snow-white, semi-double flowers with a prominent central boss of golden stamens from October through December in zone 9 — a time when few other shrubs are blooming. The flowers are lightly fragrant with a clean, sweet scent that perfumes the garden on warm fall days. The upright, arching habit reaches 8–12 feet tall with an elegant, slightly weeping branch structure that is attractive even out of bloom. Setsugekka is notably sun-tolerant compared to japonicas — it performs in full sun as well as partial shade, and more sun generally means more abundant bloom production. In zone 9, the fall and winter bloom season aligns perfectly with the region's most pleasant outdoor weather, making Setsugekka an outstanding choice for high-visibility landscape positions, espalier on walls or fences, screening, and informal hedges. The graceful branch structure also makes it an outstanding candidate for training as a small ornamental tree by gradually removing lower branches over several years.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Kanjiro blooms October–December in zone 9 — fall planting gets it established before its bloom season.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Kanjiro is one of the most popular sasanqua camellia cultivars in Gulf Coast landscapes, producing abundant, medium-sized (2–2.5-inch), semi-double flowers in a distinctive deep rose-pink to carmine-rose color from October through December in zone 9 — a color that is richer and more vivid than the typical light-pink sasanquas. The flowers are produced in overwhelming quantity on vigorous, dense, upright-to-spreading plants that reach 5–8 feet in zone 9, covering the entire plant in color for 6–8 weeks. Kanjiro is also known by the synonym 'Hiryu' in older nursery trade literature. The semi-evergreen to evergreen foliage is dark, glossy, and fine-textured, making the plant attractive even out of bloom as a hedge, screen, or foundation plant. Like all sasanqua camellias, Kanjiro is more sun-tolerant than japonicas and produces the heaviest bloom in a position receiving 4–6 hours of sun. It is also somewhat drought-tolerant once established compared to camellia japonicas. The combination of cold hardiness, heat tolerance, and prodigious bloom production makes Kanjiro one of the most dependable fall-flowering shrubs for zone 9.

common_name: Professor Sargent Camellia (Prof. Sargent Japanese Camellia)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Professor Sargent is a cold-hardy japonica camellia that blooms January–March in zone 9 — one of few flowering shrubs providing color in midwinter.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Professor Charles S. Sargent is one of the most reliably cold-hardy and long-lived Japanese camellia cultivars — a classic selection named after the founder of Harvard's Arnold Arboretum, with dark crimson-red, full peony-form flowers produced from January through March in zone 9, providing brilliant color in the depths of winter when little else is in bloom. The full peony-form flowers are large (3–4 inches), with irregular layers of ruffled, petals that give each bloom a luxuriously full, dense appearance. The plant is a strong grower reaching 8–12 feet tall with a dense, pyramidal to oval habit and outstanding glossy, dark-green foliage. Professor Sargent is considered one of the most reliably cold-hardy japonicas — surviving to zone 7 reliably and even zone 6b with protection — which made it the standard japonica for northern camellia growers for much of the 20th century. In zone 9 it thrives and produces heavy bloom loads. Japonicas bloom on old wood — never prune in winter or spring, only prune lightly immediately after bloom if needed. The midwinter bloom is vulnerable to hard freezes below 20°F that can brown open flowers.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Purple Pixie is much smaller than standard loropetalums and suits containers, low borders, and groundcover use where the standard-sized plants would be far too large.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Purple Pixie is a breakthrough compact and weeping selection of the purple-leaved loropetalum that stays only 12–24 inches tall while spreading 3.5–5 feet wide, creating a distinctive low, mounding to weeping groundcover effect entirely different from the large-growing standard selections. The deep burgundy-purple foliage is among the darkest and most richly colored of any loropetalum cultivar — maintaining its vivid color through the growing season with more sun intensity increasing the purple intensity. Bright, deep pink fringe flowers appear in spring and intermittently through the season, providing color contrast against the dark foliage. The strongly weeping branch tips cascade down and outward, creating a softening effect at wall edges, raised planting beds, and container rims that the standard upright forms cannot provide. Purple Pixie is an outstanding container plant, groundcover on slopes, and edging plant where its low profile and weeping habit can be showcased. It is Zone Award from the Gulf Coast Plant Introduction Group and performs reliably in zone 9 heat without the pest and disease problems that affect some ornamental cultivars.

common_name: Crimson Fire Loropetalum (Deep Red Fringe Flower)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Crimson Fire maintains its best leaf color intensity with 4–6 hours of direct sun — in deep shade the foliage becomes more green than purple-red.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Crimson Fire is a compact, mounding loropetalum introduced by Monrovia Nursery that combines some of the most intense foliage color in the genus — deep crimson-red to dark burgundy-red new growth that matures to a rich dark purple-red — with a manageable size of 2.5–4 feet tall and wide that fits foundation plantings and shrub borders without requiring constant shearing to control size. The deep pink to magenta fringe flowers appear in spring and intermittently through summer and fall, standing out vibrantly against the dark foliage. Crimson Fire holds its foliage color better through summer heat than some loropetalum selections, maintaining richer red-purple tones rather than fading to green-bronze. The compact size and naturally dense, mounding habit reduce the maintenance requirements that make larger loropetalums problematic in typical home foundation plantings. Loropetalums are some of the most heat- and humidity-tolerant ornamental shrubs for zone 9 and are largely free of serious pest and disease problems in the Gulf South. Provide good drainage — waterlogged soils cause rapid root rot. No pruning is required but plants respond well to light shaping immediately after the main spring bloom.
